clinicalStem: A 24-year-old woman presents with a 3-year history of nasal congestion, sneezing, and watery rhinorrhoea that worsens every spring. She also complains of itchy, watery eyes during the same period. Examination reveals pale, boggy nasal mucosa with clear discharge. She has no fever and her lungs are clear. Which single investigation would most directly confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Skin prick test with specific allergens
explanation: This patient has classic seasonal allergic rhinitis (pollinosis), characterised by seasonal sneezing, watery rhinorrhoea, nasal congestion, and ocular symptoms. Skin prick testing with specific aeroallergens is the gold-standard investigation to confirm IgE-mediated sensitisation and identify the causative allergen. A positive wheal-and-flare response confirms allergic sensitisation correlating with the clinical history.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Nasal endoscopy evaluates structural anatomy and is useful for polyps or sinusitis but does not confirm allergen sensitisation.
Serum total IgE may be elevated in allergic conditions but is non-specific; specific IgE (RAST) or skin prick test is required to identify the causative allergen.
Skin prick test with specific allergens is the correct confirmatory investigation for IgE-mediated allergic rhinitis.
CT sinuses is indicated for chronic rhinosinusitis or suspected complications, not for confirming allergic rhinitis.
Nasal cytology showing eosinophils supports allergic or NARES but does not identify the specific allergen and is not the gold standard.
clinicalPearl: Skin prick testing is the gold standard for confirming specific allergen sensitisation in allergic rhinitis — it identifies the trigger, guides avoidance, and informs immunotherapy eligibility.
keyClue: Seasonal pattern with ocular symptoms and pale boggy mucosa strongly suggests IgE-mediated allergic rhinitis requiring allergen-specific testing.
commonExamTrap: Serum total IgE is tempting but is non-specific; specific IgE or skin prick test is needed to confirm the diagnosis.
missedConcept: The distinction between total IgE (non-specific) and specific allergen testing (diagnostic) in allergic rhinitis.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old male office worker presents with perennial nasal congestion, sneezing, and profuse watery rhinorrhoea triggered by changes in temperature, strong odours, and alcohol. Symptoms have been present for 5 years with no seasonal variation. Examination shows erythematous nasal mucosa. Skin prick tests to a standard panel of aeroallergens are all negative. Serum specific IgE is undetectable. Nasal cytology reveals numerous eosinophils. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: NARES (Non-Allergic Rhinitis with Eosinophilia Syndrome) is characterised by perennial symptoms of rhinitis, negative allergy tests (negative skin prick test and undetectable specific IgE), yet prominent nasal eosinophilia on cytology. This distinguishes it from vasomotor rhinitis (no eosinophilia) and allergic rhinitis (positive allergy tests). NARES patients often respond well to topical nasal corticosteroids.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Seasonal allergic rhinitis has positive allergen sensitisation and is triggered by seasonal allergens — ruled out by negative allergy testing.
Perennial allergic rhinitis also requires positive allergen sensitisation tests; this patient has negative skin prick tests and specific IgE.
NARES fits: perennial symptoms, triggers (temperature, odours), negative allergy tests, but eosinophils on nasal cytology.
Vasomotor rhinitis shares triggers (temperature, odours) and negative allergy tests, but does NOT show nasal eosinophilia — the key distinguishing feature here.
Infectious rhinitis presents with purulent discharge, fever, and systemic symptoms, not a 5-year perennial course with eosinophilia.
clinicalPearl: NARES = perennial rhinitis + negative allergy tests + nasal eosinophilia on cytology — the eosinophilia differentiates it from vasomotor rhinitis.
keyClue: Negative allergy tests combined with nasal eosinophilia on cytology is the defining combination for NARES.
commonExamTrap: Students confuse NARES with vasomotor rhinitis; both have negative allergy tests, but only NARES has nasal eosinophilia.
missedConcept: Nasal cytology for eosinophils is the key test that differentiates NARES from vasomotor rhinitis.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman presents with severe bilateral nasal congestion for the past 6 months. She denies sneezing or rhinorrhoea. On direct questioning, she admits to using oxymetazoline nasal spray daily for the past 8 months, finding she needs it more and more frequently with shorter periods of relief. Examination shows markedly erythematous, swollen nasal mucosa bilaterally. Skin prick tests are negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound rhinitis) is caused by prolonged use of topical alpha-adrenergic decongestants such as oxymetazoline for more than 5-7 days. The result is tachyphylaxis and rebound nasal congestion on cessation. The erythematous (not pale/boggy) mucosa and negative allergy tests with clear history of prolonged decongestant use are the hallmarks.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Perennial allergic rhinitis features sneezing, rhinorrhoea, pale boggy mucosa, and positive allergy tests — absent here.
Chronic rhinosinusitis typically includes facial pain, pressure, nasal discharge, and reduced sense of smell for over 12 weeks.
Rhinitis medicamentosa is the correct diagnosis given >5-7 days of topical decongestant use causing rebound congestion.
Nasal polyposis presents with bilateral obstruction and hyposmia but is associated with pale grape-like masses on endoscopy, not decongestant use.
Deviated nasal septum causes unilateral obstruction and is a structural finding, not related to decongestant use.
clinicalPearl: Any patient with worsening nasal congestion who uses topical decongestants daily for >1 week should be suspected of rhinitis medicamentosa — always ask about nasal spray use.
keyClue: 8 months of daily oxymetazoline use with progressive dependence and erythematous mucosa is the hallmark of rhinitis medicamentosa.
commonExamTrap: Students may label this allergic rhinitis; the erythematous mucosa (not pale/boggy) and negative allergy tests exclude it.
missedConcept: Rhinitis medicamentosa is caused by topical decongestant overuse, not allergen sensitisation — medication history is essential.

subtopic: Clinical criteria for bacterial vs viral
clinicalStem: A 32-year-old man presents with nasal congestion and facial pain that began 10 days ago following a cold. He initially improved after 5 days but then worsened with increasing left-sided facial pressure, purulent nasal discharge, and fever of 38.4°C. Examination shows tenderness over the left maxillary sinus and purulent discharge in the middle meatus. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: The key feature distinguishing acute bacterial from viral rhinosinusitis is the 'double sickening' pattern — initial improvement followed by worsening — along with symptoms persisting beyond 10 days, fever, and purulent discharge. This case fulfils the EPOS criteria for acute bacterial rhinosinusitis. Viral sinusitis typically resolves within 10 days without this biphasic course.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Viral rhinosinusitis peaks at 2-3 days and resolves within 10 days; this patient had initial improvement then worsening — the double sickening sign for bacterial infection.
Allergic rhinosinusitis presents with sneezing, watery discharge, and seasonal pattern — not fever and purulent discharge.
Acute bacterial rhinosinusitis is correct: double sickening, >10 days duration, fever, purulent discharge, and maxillary tenderness.
Chronic rhinosinusitis is defined by symptoms lasting >12 weeks; this is an acute presentation.
Dental abscess can cause maxillary sinusitis but would also present with dental pain and there is no dental history here.
clinicalPearl: Double sickening (initial improvement then worsening after day 5), symptoms >10 days, fever, and purulent discharge are the cardinal features distinguishing acute bacterial from viral rhinosinusitis.
keyClue: Initial improvement followed by worsening (double sickening) with fever and purulent discharge beyond 10 days indicates bacterial superinfection.
commonExamTrap: Assuming any sinus-related symptom after a cold is viral; double sickening is a red flag for bacterial conversion.
missedConcept: The clinical criteria for acute bacterial rhinosinusitis: double sickening, >10 days duration, or severe symptoms from onset with fever and unilateral pain.

clinicalStem: A 10-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with a 4-day history of right-sided facial pain, nasal congestion, and fever. Over the past 24 hours, his parents noticed right eyelid swelling, redness, and he is now complaining of pain on eye movement. His visual acuity is reduced in the right eye. He appears toxic. Vital signs: T 39.2°C, HR 112, BP 100/65. What is the most important immediate investigation?
options: CT scan of orbit and sinuses with contrast
explanation: This child has signs of orbital complication of acute rhinosinusitis — eyelid oedema, proptosis, ophthalmoplegia (pain on eye movement), and reduced visual acuity, indicating orbital cellulitis or subperiosteal/orbital abscess. Contrast-enhanced CT of the orbit and sinuses is the investigation of choice to delineate the extent of spread, identify an abscess requiring surgical drainage, and confirm the diagnosis. This is a surgical emergency.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Plain X-ray has poor sensitivity for sinus disease and cannot assess orbital soft tissue or abscess — inadequate for this emergency.
Nasal endoscopy assesses intranasal anatomy but cannot evaluate orbital extension or abscess formation.
Contrast CT of orbit and sinuses is the correct and urgent investigation — it identifies subperiosteal or orbital abscess and guides surgical planning.
MRI provides better soft-tissue detail but takes longer and is more difficult in an acutely unwell child; CT is the first-line urgent investigation.
Orbital ultrasound has limited penetration and cannot reliably assess subperiosteal abscess or sinus disease.
clinicalPearl: Reduced visual acuity + pain on eye movement + proptosis in sinusitis = presume orbital abscess until proven otherwise — urgent contrast CT and surgical consultation are mandatory.
keyClue: Reduced visual acuity with pain on eye movement in the context of acute sinusitis indicates orbital extension requiring urgent CT.
commonExamTrap: Ordering plain X-rays in an emergency — they are inadequate for assessing orbital complications and waste critical time.
missedConcept: Orbital complications of sinusitis (Chandler classification) require urgent CT to determine if an abscess needs surgical drainage.
redFlagReason: Reduced visual acuity with ophthalmoplegia in acute sinusitis indicates orbital abscess — a sight-threatening surgical emergency requiring immediate CT and ENT/ophthalmology referral.

subtopic: Role of imaging in uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis
clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 7-day history of nasal congestion, facial pressure, and green nasal discharge following an upper respiratory tract infection. There is no fever above 38°C, no periorbital swelling, no neck stiffness, and no change in vision. Examination is unremarkable except for tenderness over the maxillary sinuses bilaterally. She asks whether she needs an X-ray or scan of her sinuses. What is the most appropriate response?
options: Arrange plain X-ray of the sinuses to confirm the diagnosis
Arrange CT sinuses to guide antibiotic choice
Arrange MRI sinuses to exclude tumour
No imaging is required for uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis
Arrange ultrasound of the maxillary sinuses
explanation: Current guidelines (EPOS) state that imaging is NOT recommended in uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis. The diagnosis is clinical. Plain X-rays have poor sensitivity and specificity, and CT is reserved for complications (orbital or intracranial spread) or planned surgery. Ordering imaging in uncomplicated cases does not change management and exposes the patient to unnecessary radiation or cost.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Plain X-rays of sinuses are not recommended in guidelines due to poor sensitivity/specificity and do not alter management in uncomplicated cases.
CT sinuses is indicated for suspected complications or pre-surgical planning, not for uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis.
MRI sinuses is not indicated for acute rhinosinusitis and is only used in specific situations such as fungal sinusitis or intracranial complications.
No imaging is the correct answer: guidelines recommend clinical diagnosis and symptomatic management for uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis.
Ultrasound of the maxillary sinuses has limited evidence, is rarely used clinically, and is not part of standard guidelines for acute rhinosinusitis.
clinicalPearl: Imaging is not routinely indicated in uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis — it is a clinical diagnosis; reserve CT for suspected complications or pre-operative planning.
keyClue: No red flag features (no orbital signs, no meningism, no fever >38.5°C) means this is uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis — imaging is not required.
commonExamTrap: Reflexively ordering imaging for sinusitis — only do so for complications or pre-surgical cases.
missedConcept: Guidelines recommend no imaging in uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis; CT is only for complications or planned FESS.

clinicalStem: A 50-year-old man presents with a 14-week history of bilateral nasal blockage, thick mucopurulent discharge, facial pressure, and loss of smell. He denies allergy history. Anterior rhinoscopy shows mucopurulent discharge. He has tried two courses of antibiotics with only partial improvement. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) is defined by the presence of two or more symptoms (nasal blockage, discharge, facial pain/pressure, or reduced smell) lasting more than 12 weeks, with objective evidence of inflammation. This patient has bilateral blockage, mucopurulent discharge, facial pressure, and hyposmia for 14 weeks — meeting the EPOS criteria for CRS without polyps (no polyps on examination). Anterior rhinoscopy confirms discharge.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Acute bacterial rhinosinusitis lasts less than 12 weeks; 14-week history excludes this diagnosis.
Allergic rhinitis features sneezing, watery discharge, and nasal itching with positive allergy tests — this patient has thick mucopurulent discharge and no allergy history.
CRS without nasal polyps is correct: >12 weeks, bilateral symptoms, mucopurulent discharge, no polyps on examination.
Nasal polyposis is also a subtype of CRS but requires visualisation of polyps; no polyps are noted on examination here.
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis causes nasal crusting, septal perforation, and epistaxis with systemic features — not described here.
clinicalPearl: CRS is defined as nasal symptoms (two or more: blockage, discharge, facial pain, hyposmia) lasting >12 weeks with objective evidence of inflammation — 12 weeks is the key time threshold.
keyClue: 14-week duration of bilateral nasal obstruction, mucopurulent discharge, facial pressure, and hyposmia fulfils the EPOS definition of chronic rhinosinusitis.
commonExamTrap: Confusing CRS with prolonged acute sinusitis — the 12-week threshold is the diagnostic criterion for chronicity.
missedConcept: CRS requires ≥2 symptoms for >12 weeks with objective evidence (examination, nasal endoscopy, or CT).

clinicalStem: A 42-year-old woman with a 6-month history of bilateral nasal obstruction, thick greenish discharge, and anosmia attends the ENT clinic. Clinical examination and anterior rhinoscopy are limited due to oedematous turbinates. Her GP has already prescribed two courses of antibiotics and a nasal steroid spray with minimal improvement. Which investigation should be performed first to objectively confirm the diagnosis and assess the extent of disease?
options: Nasal smear for culture and sensitivity
explanation: Nasal endoscopy (rigid or flexible) is the first-line objective investigation in chronic rhinosinusitis. It directly visualises the nasal cavity, middle meatus, discharge, polyps, and mucosal changes, providing objective confirmation of CRS and essential information about disease extent. CT sinuses is reserved for cases that fail medical therapy and are being considered for surgical intervention (FESS), not as the initial investigation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CT sinuses is the investigation of choice when planning surgery (FESS) but is not the initial investigation in CRS — nasal endoscopy comes first.
MRI sinuses is used for fungal sinusitis, tumours, or intracranial complications — not routine CRS investigation.
Nasal endoscopy is the correct first-line objective investigation: it directly visualises disease, confirms CRS, and identifies polyps or purulence in the middle meatus.
Allergy testing may be appropriate if allergic aetiology is suspected but does not confirm or assess the extent of CRS.
Nasal culture is occasionally used to guide antibiotic therapy but does not confirm CRS diagnosis or assess disease extent.
clinicalPearl: Nasal endoscopy is the first-line objective investigation in CRS — CT sinuses is reserved for pre-surgical planning when medical therapy has failed.
keyClue: Anterior rhinoscopy is limited by turbinate oedema — nasal endoscopy provides direct visualisation of the middle meatus and confirms CRS.
commonExamTrap: Jumping to CT sinuses as the first investigation — guidelines recommend nasal endoscopy first, with CT reserved for surgical planning.
missedConcept: The investigative hierarchy in CRS: clinical diagnosis first, nasal endoscopy to confirm, CT only if surgery is planned.

topic: CRS with nasal polyps — associated conditions
clinicalStem: A 36-year-old man with a history of difficult-to-control asthma presents with progressive bilateral nasal obstruction and anosmia over 18 months. He is found to have bilateral nasal polyps on endoscopy. On further questioning, he reports developing facial swelling, urticaria, and severe bronchospasm after taking ibuprofen for back pain 2 years ago. He now avoids all NSAIDs. What is the most likely unifying diagnosis?
explanation: Samter's triad (Aspirin-Exacerbated Respiratory Disease, AERD) consists of the classic triad of: asthma, nasal polyposis, and hypersensitivity to aspirin/NSAIDs. The reaction is non-IgE-mediated and results from cyclooxygenase-1 inhibition causing excess leukotriene production. This patient has all three components: asthma, bilateral nasal polyps with anosmia, and a severe reaction to ibuprofen.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Allergic fungal rhinosinusitis features eosinophilic mucin, unilateral or asymmetric polyps, and characteristic CT findings — no NSAID hypersensitivity is described.
Cystic fibrosis causes nasal polyps and can present in adults but is associated with recurrent chest infections, bronchiectasis, and malabsorption — not NSAID hypersensitivity.
Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (Samter's triad) is correct: asthma + nasal polyps + NSAID hypersensitivity.
Churg-Strauss syndrome (EGPA) involves eosinophilic vasculitis with systemic features (skin, nerve, heart) and asthma — the NSAID trigger is not a defining feature.
Kartagener's syndrome features bronchiectasis, situs inversus, and sinusitis due to ciliary dysfunction — no asthma or NSAID hypersensitivity.
clinicalPearl: The triad of asthma + nasal polyps + NSAID hypersensitivity = Samter's triad (AERD) — always ask about NSAID reactions in patients with severe nasal polyposis.
keyClue: Severe bronchospasm following ibuprofen in a patient with asthma and bilateral nasal polyps is pathognomonic of Samter's triad.
commonExamTrap: Confusing AERD with an IgE-mediated drug allergy — NSAID hypersensitivity in AERD is pharmacological (COX-1 inhibition), not allergic.
missedConcept: Samter's triad is a non-IgE-mediated condition caused by COX-1 inhibition leading to excess leukotriene production — aspirin/NSAID challenge is diagnostic.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old man presents with a 2-year history of progressive bilateral nasal obstruction and complete loss of smell. He denies pain or epistaxis. Anterior rhinoscopy reveals bilateral pale, glistening, grape-like masses that are insensitive to probing. They arise from the middle meatus. He has a background of chronic rhinosinusitis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Nasal polyps are benign inflammatory outgrowths from the nasal mucosa (most commonly the middle meatus) presenting as pale, grey, glistening, insensitive (no pain on probing — no blood supply or nerve supply), bilateral masses causing progressive obstruction and anosmia. The bilateral nature, pale appearance, insensitivity to probing, and middle meatal origin distinguish them from turbinates and malignancy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Turbinate hypertrophy presents as pink/red, sensitive mucosal swellings that shrink with decongestants — not pale insensitive grape-like masses.
Nasal polyps are the correct diagnosis: bilateral, pale, glistening, insensitive, arising from the middle meatus with progressive obstruction and anosmia.
Antrochoanal polyp is typically unilateral, arises from the maxillary sinus, and extends to the nasopharynx — not bilateral middle meatus masses.
Inverted papilloma is usually unilateral, has a cerebriform surface on MRI, carries malignant potential, and is not pale/insensitive grape-like masses.
Nasal lymphoma presents as an aggressive mass with associated systemic features (fever, weight loss, lymphadenopathy) — not a chronic bilateral pale polypoid appearance.
clinicalPearl: Nasal polyps are pale, glistening, insensitive to probing, and arise bilaterally from the middle meatus — insensitivity distinguishes them from turbinates and malignancy.
keyClue: Pale, glistening, insensitive grape-like masses arising bilaterally from the middle meatus = nasal polyps.
commonExamTrap: Confusing nasal polyps with turbinate hypertrophy — probe the mass: polyps are insensitive while turbinates are sensitive.
missedConcept: Insensitivity to probing (no nerve or significant blood supply) is a classic clinical feature that distinguishes polyps from turbinates.

topic: Nasal polyps in children — associated diagnosis
clinicalStem: An 8-year-old boy is referred to ENT with bilateral nasal polyps confirmed on endoscopy. He has a history of recurrent chest infections and loose, greasy stools. His BMI is on the 3rd centile. What is the single most important investigation to perform in this child?
explanation: Nasal polyps in a child are rare and should prompt investigation for cystic fibrosis (CF) until proven otherwise. CF is caused by CFTR mutations leading to thick secretions affecting lungs, pancreas, and sinuses. The sweat chloride test (Gibson and Cooke method) is the gold-standard screening/diagnostic test for CF. The associated recurrent chest infections, malabsorption (greasy stools), and failure to thrive strongly support this diagnosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Allergy testing is appropriate for suspected allergic rhinitis but nasal polyps in a child first mandate excluding cystic fibrosis.
CT sinuses may be needed for surgical planning but is not the most important investigation when CF is the primary concern.
Sweat chloride test is the gold standard for cystic fibrosis — the correct answer given nasal polyps + recurrent chest infections + malabsorption in a child.
Serum IgE assesses allergy or ABPA but does not diagnose CF, which is the priority diagnosis here.
Nasal cytology provides information about eosinophilia or infection but does not diagnose the underlying systemic condition in this child.
clinicalPearl: Nasal polyps in a child are a red flag for cystic fibrosis — always arrange a sweat chloride test as the first investigation.
keyClue: Bilateral nasal polyps in a child + recurrent chest infections + malabsorption = cystic fibrosis until proven otherwise; sweat chloride test is diagnostic.
commonExamTrap: Treating paediatric nasal polyps as idiopathic CRS like in adults — in children, CF must be excluded first.
missedConcept: Bilateral nasal polyps in children are rare and should trigger CF screening; in adults, they are most commonly associated with CRS and allergy.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old male smoker presents with a 3-month history of progressive unilateral (right-sided) nasal obstruction, blood-stained discharge, and right-sided facial pain. Anterior rhinoscopy reveals a fleshy, irregular, bleeding mass arising from the right nasal cavity. He has no polyp history and no allergy. What is the single most important next investigation?
options: Biopsy of the nasal mass under endoscopic guidance
Trial of intranasal corticosteroids for 4 weeks
explanation: A unilateral nasal mass in an elderly smoker with blood-stained discharge, facial pain, and an irregular bleeding appearance is malignant until proven otherwise. Sinonasal malignancies (squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, inverted papilloma with malignant transformation) present this way. Endoscopic-guided biopsy is the essential investigation to obtain histological diagnosis. Never trial steroids or delay biopsy on a unilateral irregular bleeding mass.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Allergy testing is irrelevant here — this is not an allergic presentation; malignancy must be excluded urgently.
Nasal swab for culture only identifies infection and will not diagnose a neoplastic mass.
Biopsy under endoscopic guidance is the correct urgent investigation — histological diagnosis is essential to exclude malignancy in any unilateral irregular nasal mass.
Sinus X-rays have poor sensitivity and cannot characterise soft tissue masses or confirm histology.
A trial of intranasal corticosteroids is appropriate for bilateral polyps but is contraindicated as a first step for a unilateral bleeding mass — it delays diagnosis of potential malignancy.
clinicalPearl: Unilateral nasal mass + blood-stained discharge + facial pain in an elderly smoker = malignancy until proven otherwise — urgent biopsy is mandatory, never trial steroids.
keyClue: Unilateral, irregular, bleeding nasal mass in an elderly smoker is a red flag for sinonasal malignancy requiring urgent biopsy.
commonExamTrap: Treating a unilateral irregular nasal mass as inflammatory polyp with steroids — this delays diagnosis of potentially curable malignancy.
missedConcept: Unilateral nasal masses are red flags; bilateral presentation is more consistent with benign polyps. Any unilateral mass needs biopsy to exclude malignancy.
redFlagReason: Unilateral bleeding nasal mass in an elderly smoker is high risk for sinonasal malignancy — delayed diagnosis is a serious oncological harm; urgent biopsy is required.
